codeReview.summary: Wu's anti-aliased line algorithm correctly replaces Bresenham. The implementation handles all required cases: single point, axis-aligned (pixel-perfect), 45-degree (no fringe), and general diagonal (coverage-based AA). The algorithm structure is sound — steep/shallow decomposition, left-to-right normalization, fixed-point gradient, and per-pixel coverage blending via the existing gamma-correct sRGB infrastructure. 9 well-targeted tests cover all expectedBehavior items and validation assertions (VAL-PRIM-009, VAL-PRIM-010). Two non-blocking code quality issues found.

issues.description: Dead code: `let y_int = y_fp >> 8;` is immediately shadowed by the following `let y_int = if y_fp < 0 { ... }` on the next line. The first assignment is never read. Moreover, Rust's `>>` on i32 is an arithmetic right shift (sign-extending), so `y_fp >> 8` already produces the correct floor-division result for negative values — the explicit branch that shadows it is equivalent but less efficient. Either remove the dead first line or remove the redundant branch and keep only `y_fp >> 8`.

Half-pixel y-intercept bias: `y_fp = ay0 * 256 + 128 + gradient_fp` adds a +128 (0.5px) bias to the initial y-intercept. Standard Wu's algorithm initializes at `y0 + gradient` (no 0.5 bias). The +128 shifts coverage values so that a line mathematically passing through a pixel center gets split coverage (50/50) instead of full coverage on one pixel. This creates a subtle 0.5px displacement of the line center relative to the mathematical line, and a minor discontinuity between the solid endpoint pixels (drawn at exact integer positions by wu_endpoint) and the first interior AA pixel. The current tests don't catch this because they check for presence of intermediate alpha values and total coverage, not exact coverage distribution. Visually the effect is very subtle for most angles. Non-blocking because the line looks correct and all tests pass, but worth noting for future precision work.

Unrelated change bundled in commit: Node size assertion updated from 72 to 60 bytes. This is a correction from a previous commit (likely the path-rendering or scene refactor) but doesn't belong in the antialiased-lines feature commit. Minor scope creep — the commit message doesn't mention this change.

sharedStateObservations.observation: The axis-aligned line fast paths use set_pixel (direct write, no alpha blending) while diagonal line paths use blend_pixel (source-over compositing). For fully opaque colors (a=255) this is equivalent since blend_pixel delegates to set_pixel. For semi-transparent colors, axis-aligned lines would stamp without blending while diagonal lines would blend. This matches the original Bresenham behavior (also used set_pixel) so it's not a regression, but the drawing library has no documented convention on whether draw_line should blend or stamp for semi-transparent colors. AGENTS.md says 'Gamma-correct sRGB blending is mandatory for all alpha compositing' but doesn't clarify whether line drawing counts as compositing when the color itself is semi-transparent.
sharedStateObservations.evidence: system/libraries/drawing/lib.rs: axis-aligned paths at lines ~555-570 use self.set_pixel(), wu_plot at line ~666 uses self.blend_pixel(), wu_endpoint at line ~682 uses self.blend_pixel(). Original Bresenham also used set_pixel.

sharedStateObservations.observation: Worker followed the os-worker skill procedure correctly: read feature and source, wrote tests first (TDD), implemented, ran full test suite, built kernel. The skill says to do visual verification 'if display pipeline changed' — the worker correctly skipped visual verification since draw_line is not currently used in the compositor display pipeline (only in tests). skillFeedback confirms followedProcedure: true with no deviations.
sharedStateObservations.evidence: Handoff verification shows cargo test and cargo build --release both passing. Transcript skeleton shows worker grepped for draw_line callers and confirmed it's only used in tests.

summary: Wu's anti-aliased line algorithm is a correct and well-tested replacement for Bresenham. The implementation cleanly handles all required cases: pixel-perfect axis-aligned lines, smooth diagonal edges with intermediate coverage, consistent visual width, no gaps, and directional symmetry. Two non-blocking issues found: (1) a dead-code line where y_int is computed then immediately shadowed by an equivalent but more verbose computation, and (2) a +128 half-pixel bias in the y-intercept initialization that subtly shifts coverage distribution but doesn't affect visual quality for most use cases. 9 new tests comprehensively cover the feature's expectedBehavior and validation assertions. All 1672 tests pass. Pass.
